title: Continuous-time quantum control across an exponentially small bottleneck in a frustrated Ising ring model
authors: Vincenzo Roberto Arezzo, Kiran Thengil, Giuseppe Santoro
abstract: Continuous-time Quantum Annealing (QA) is a strategy for preparing the ground state of nontrivial many-body systems. In its standard form, the dynamics is generated by a time-dependent interpolation between a simple driving Hamiltonian and the target problem Hamiltonian, usually implemented through a linear schedule. This approach faces the crucial bottleneck of small spectral gaps, which may require exponentially long annealing times to ensure adiabaticity. Here, we show how to implement quantum control over the annealing schedule in a frustrated Ising ring, one of the simplest models exhibiting an exponentially small bottleneck gap. By optimizing smooth continuous-time annealing schedules with a dressed-CRAB approach, and using a digitized representation of the dynamics to efficiently evaluate gradients, we construct protocols that strongly outperform standard fixed schedules. The optimized dynamics bypasses the bottleneck through a strongly nonadiabatic mechanism, leading to efficient ground-state preparation despite the exponentially small minimum gap. In particular, the annealing time required to reach a fixed residual-energy threshold is found to grow linearly with system size rather than exponentially. We further examine a lowest-order variational counter-diabatic correction and find that, once schedule optimization is allowed, it does not lead to any improvement.
summary: This paper develops advanced quantum control techniques to prepare the ground state of a frustrated Ising ring model, which typically suffers from exponentially small energy gaps. By optimizing the annealing schedule continuously rather than using fixed ramps, the authors demonstrate that the required computation time scales only linearly with system size. This provides a significant theoretical breakthrough for simulating complex many-body systems trapped by spectral bottlenecks.
main_problem: The primary challenge is overcoming exponentially long annealing times required by standard Quantum Annealing (QA) when the system encounters an exponentially small spectral gap bottleneck.
main_result: By optimizing continuous-time control schedules, the required annealing time scales linearly with system size rather than exponentially, successfully bypassing the bottleneck mechanism.
method: The authors use quantum optimal control theory to optimize smooth, continuous-time annealing schedules using a dressed-CRAB approach and gradient optimization techniques.
model_or_system: The study focuses on a frustrated Ising ring model, whose dynamics are governed by an interpolating Hamiltonian between a driving field ($\hat{H}_x$) and the target problem ($\hat{H}_z$).
key_observables: Residual energy ($\epsilon$), threshold annealing time ($	au^*$), and population distributions across instantaneous eigenstates.
important_parameters: System size ($N$), minimum spectral gap ($\Delta_{\min}$), and the optimization schedule parameters (Fourier coefficients).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on optimizing smooth, continuous-time schedules; counter-diabatic corrections are analyzed but found not to improve results beyond optimal scheduling.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the frustrated Ising ring structure, show population dynamics near bottlenecks, and compare residual energies for optimized vs. fixed annealing schedules across varying system sizes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QA problem on the frustrated Ising ring, details the continuous-time control optimization using dressed-CRAB, analyzes the resulting nonadiabatic dynamics (population transfer), compares performance against standard ramps, and finally examines the role of counter-diabatic corrections.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses fundamental limitations in quantum simulation algorithms by providing a concrete method to achieve polynomial scaling for ground state preparation in systems with known spectral bottlenecks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The Ising frustrated ring model with an odd number of sites N. The model has a reflection symmetry around the axis indicated by the dashed blue line.

FIG. 2: Comparison between optimized continuous-time residual energies and fixed-schedule results for N = 13, Jf = 0.45 (a,c), and N = 35, Jf = 0.45 (b,d). Panels (a) and (b) show the geometric mean of the optimized residual energies ¯ϵP(τ) over Nrun = 10 independent dressed-CRAB runs. The shaded regions indicate one logarithmic standard deviation. Panels (c) and (d) show representative optimized schedules sopt(t) for the case Nc = 20, and several values of τ.

FIG. 3: Threshold annealing time τ ∗extracted from the con- dition ¯ϵP(τ) < ϵthreshold = 10−6, as a function of the system size N. For each N, we scan the annealing time τ in steps of ∆τ = 1 and define τ ∗as the smallest scanned value of τ for which the geometric mean of the optimized residual en- ergy satisfies ¯ϵP(τ) < ϵthreshold. The data are shown in log-log scale. The dashed line is a linear-scaling guide to the eye, τ ∗∝N, normalized using the largest system size. The com- parison suggests that, in the range of sizes explored here, the threshold time τ ∗is compatible with a linear scaling with N. All data are obtained with the default two-iteration dressed- CRAB setup described in...

FIG. 4: Populations of the low-lying instantaneous eigenstates during the optimized dynamics for two system sizes: N = 13 at τ = 60 (panel (a)) and N = 35 at τ = 120 (panel (b)). All data are obtained with the default two-iteration dressed-CRAB setup described in Sec. III. Notice the abrupt final population transfer for values of t/τ close to the bottleneck value of sb ≈0.9. The final ground-state infidelities are 1 −p0(τ) ≃3.8 × 10−8 for N = 13 and 1 −p0(τ) ≃9.6 × 10−7 for N = 35.

FIG. 5: Comparison of the optimized residual energies obtained with the standard and counter-diabatic protocols, together with the results for fixed linear, cubic, and sinusoidal schedules. The data refer to Jf = 0.2501 and N = 17 in panels (a,c), and to Jf = 0.2501 and N = 33 in panels (b,d). The residual-energy points show the geometric mean ¯ϵP(τ) over Nrun = 10 independent dressed-CRAB runs, while the shaded regions indicate one logarithmic standard deviation. The bottom panels compare the optimized schedules for τ = 80 and show the corresponding profile of α(s) ˙s(t) entering the counter-diabatic correction.
